# Startup Archive’s Post
[![View organization page for Startup Archive]()](https://www.linkedin.com/company/the-startup-archive?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)
[Startup Archive](https://www.linkedin.com/company/the-startup-archive?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)
31,424 followers
3mo
Patrick Collison on the importance of waiting a really long time to hire people
Everybody tells you “work really hard to hire the best people.” But as Patrick explains, that’s not helpful because everyone knows they should do that. As he puts it:
“The question is to what length should you go and what does that actually mean in practice? And in practice, it means being okay waiting a really long time to hire people.”
It took Stripe six months to hire their first two people, and six more months to hire another 3-4 people. He can think of five people at Stripe who took 3+ years to hire.
“If you think about the smartest people you know, if you want to get them to work on your thing, chances are they already have pretty good paths ahead of them… You have to be way more persistent and be okay with it taking way longer than any sane or reasonable person would think it should take.”
There’s an important compounding effect here —hiring just one great person will make it marginally easier to get the next great person. Patrick argues you should also view every person you hire as bringing along another 50 people just like them if your company is successful:
“Even if they don’t literally hire 50 people, they will be so influential in determining the selection of those 50 people.”
Video source:[Greylock](https://www.linkedin.com/company/greylock-partners?trk=public_post-text)(2015)

TranscriptTranscript
##### **Transcript**
Received wisdom is worked really hard to hire the best people and you're like, Gee, thanks. You know, I was going to do the opposite. But now you've said that work really hard to hire the best people. And so I guess the question is sort of what length should you go and kind of what does it actually mean in practice? And in practice it means being OK waiting a really long time to hire people. It took me 6 months to hire the first two people, so a person per quarter, even though we had this kind of network already and you all these people, we've managed like one every three months and then in I think the six months after that. We may be hired another. Three or four people kind of on that order, yeah. You just think about of the smartest people you know and you want to get them to work on your thing. I mean, chances are as far as people you know are working already, yeah, they like they have like pretty good paths out of them, right? There were multiple people that stripe now today who took us several years to hire. I can think off the top of my head of maybe five people who took three plus years to hire. The big thing that people have to keep in mind instead of early stage company recruiting is just you have to be way more persistent. To be OK with taking way longer than any sane or reasonable person you know could think it should take. The vicious cycle is that you're going so slowly when it's the first two or three people and you're like, holy smokes, if we could just get another set of hands and you know, but you don't want to do it. But we talk about this class about compound interest and the value of compounding interest. Like if you get just one great person, right, that makes it marginally easier to get the next great person. So I think that dynamic in spades. And then kind of secondly, I think another way to think about it makes the kind of the importance of it and the potency. That tier is that you have to kind of imagine the tree of sort of all the people this person will hire and going rather than just think about it as kind of do do I want this person or not? It's like, do I want this person and the 50 people who I think they will hire? Because even if they don't literally hire 50 people, they will be kind of so influential in determining sort of the self selection of those 50 people.

Founder, Leap | Previously, Elevation Capital| My posts are about business models, productivity and startups
3moEdited
We have this joke at Leap - "Speed as a habit - in everything except hiring"
We want to hire slow
As we grow older and wiser as a company, we are getting slower in hiring
The speed of hiring is inversely correlated with the quality of hires you make
It is absolutely okay to wait a really really long time to hire the right person.
And the worst hiring mistakes we have made are the ones where the hiring manager was tired of searching for 2 months and hired the first passably good candidate out of exhaustion and desperation.
Classical sunk cost and local maxima optimisation scenario
Hire slow
Never hire out of exhaustion
Hire Bar-Raisers
Wait for a long time for the right hire

🚀The Hidden Superpower of Great Hiring: Patience 🕰️Everyone says, "Hire the best people." But as[Stripe](https://www.linkedin.com/company/stripe?trk=public_post-text)'s[Patrick Collison](https://www.linkedin.com/in/patrickcollison?trk=public_post-text)highlights, that's not the real insight. The game-changer? Understanding what it truly takes to hire the best. And spoiler: it’s a lot more patience than you think.
Here’s Patrick’s perspective:
💡“The question is to what length should you go and what does that actually mean in practice? In practice, it means being okay waiting a really long time to hire people.”
💼At Stripe, it took 6 months to hire their first two employees. Another 6 months to find the next 3-4. Patrick even recalls 5 hires that took over 3 years of persistence to land.
Why? Because the smartest, most capable people often have great opportunities lined up. To bring them into your vision, you have to go further than most would —more persistence, more patience, more long-term thinking.
But here’s the payoff: Compounding talent.
Every great hire doesn’t just add value. They become a magnet for more greatness. Patrick says:
💬“Even if they don’t literally hire 50 people, they will be so influential in determining the selection of those 50 people.”
🔥Takeaway: Building an extraordinary team isn’t about speed; it’s about compounding excellence through relentless patience and unwavering vision.
💬Community Call: Who’s the smartest hire you’ve ever made, and how did you make it happen? Share your story and inspire others! 👇[#Startups](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/startups&trk=public_post-text)[#Leadership](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/leadership&trk=public_post-text)[#Recruitment](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/recruitment&trk=public_post-text)[#Innovation](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/innovation&trk=public_post-text)[#PatrickCollison](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/patrickcollison&trk=public_post-text)[#Stripe](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/stripe&trk=public_post-text)[#VentureBuilding](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/venturebuilding&trk=public_post-text)

What keeps every tech founder awake at night?
When tech founders share what keeps them up at night, you often hear things like Funding, Market Competition, Customer Acquisition, Scalability
For me, it's different.
What keeps me awake is the hunt for a developer who gets my business and can help it grow. After two weeks of searching, I was tired.
Recently, while scrolling through Google, I discovered Gigson and decided to give it a try.
Just a few days later, I onboarded my first developer, and now I'm about to bring on my third. Gigson made it incredibly easy to build my dev team. They quickly filled my vacancies, and the entire process was seamless and efficient.
Now, I can finally focus on what truly matters: growing my business. With Gigson as my tech hiring partner, I have more time to innovate and drive my vision forward.
Start reducing developer hiring time here:[https://bit.ly/3KWjVN5](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://bit.ly/3KWjVN5&urlhash=788d&trk=public_post-text)[#Gigson](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/gigson&trk=public_post-text)[#techfounders](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/techfounders&trk=public_post-text)[#hiring](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/hiring&trk=public_post-text)[#developers](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/developers&trk=public_post-text)

3mo
I'm convinced that most tech companies are massively overstaffed. Yet the recent announcements of multiple tech companies to fire 5% of "low-performers" ultimately get it backward.
Overstaffing needs to be fixed with better (and less) hiring and not "better" firing!
Firing 5% of "low-performers" means a) you need to come up with metrics for rating your employees which leads to b) your workforce focusing on gaming those metrics (Goodhart's law).
And don't get me started on natural performance fluctuations that happen in the life of humans (e.g., because of the death of a loved one, break-ups, childbirth,...).
Hire when it hurts, and fire when it really hurts, but not sooner!
````

Former : Fivetran, Yelp, Demandforce, Illumio, Lavender 💜5moEdited
Saw a post regarding a trend in hiring with Startups having a "No Bay Area" Sales talent post recently. Hey Pete - you can't stop saying things that get you onto r/LinkedinLunatics. Your post is super insulting - San Francisco built Silicon Valley. I would bet, most founders implementing this rule happens to be someone who wants more bargaining leverage in the pay to employee exchange by hiring lower cost talent. With probably a couple other things internal council has told them they can pickup for free by "off California'ing" their sales teams. The real sales talent is in the Bay Area - we've been selling software for over a decade and did it at companies like Yelp or DemandForce with products that haven't changed in 15 years. And growth stage startups - You're very smart about some things, but you post really insulting things about the people who create shareholder value, WFH, RTO, and now you're advocating geographical discrimination.... [Ahmed Yassin bettaib](https://tn.linkedin.com/in/ahmedyasiinfromtruggl?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)
Executive Director @ Truggl.video | Animated sales videos to sell | B2B SaaS, Fintech, Legal, HealthTech
5moEdited
We experienced a spike in revenue from the middle of August until today.
Funny enough, if you open our Stripe, it's the first thing you'll notice. 😂How did that happen?
I put arrogance aside and turned it off.
I started receiving a lot of feedback. And when I say a lot, I mean an annoyingly large amount. I ask clients about everything where, when, and how and I record those conversations to share with my team.
And if you think it stopped there, no, it didn't. It went way further.
I know some great people—really great entrepreneurs and marketing experts.
Some of them were my clients, some were friends, and some were just acquaintances.
I actually get on calls or meet with them, telling them everything imaginable about my business:
- Where we are at
- What we are planning to do
- How we are planning to do it
I genuinely enjoy listening to their feedback and taking notes.
Some of them said, "Then why aren't you recording content?"
So, now I record content four times a month.
Some suggested, "Why don't you hire more people?"
So, I started hiring like crazy.
Others even pointed out, "You have a problem in your system—why do you make clients wait for a quote?"
As a result, we added a pricing page at[truggl.com/pricing](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http://truggl.com/pricing&urlhash=62C3&trk=public_post-text).
I always take that feedback positively, even if it feels offensive, and use it to grow my business.
I even get messages from young people asking how I do what I do. I tell them it’s simple: you're young, inexperienced, and have nothing to lose.
Open your laptop, learn something, post content every day even if you see no immediate results, sell your services, and congratulations—you’re on the path I took.
(And when I say 'kids,' I mean people my age 😅).
---[truggl.video](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http://truggl.video&urlhash=LlZs&trk=public_post-text)for great videos.

Patrick Collison’s Leadership Turned Stripe Into a $95B Payments Juggernaut. I spent weeks breaking down his interviews, culture docs, and growth playbooks. The result? A masterclass in leadership.… | Mika Tikkala

# Mika Tikkala’s Post
Mika Tikkala
1y
-
Patrick Collison’s Leadership Turned Stripe Into a $95B Payments Juggernaut. I spent weeks breaking down his interviews, culture docs, and growth playbooks. The result? A masterclass in leadership. Here’s how Patrick’s philosophy turned Stripe into a global payments backbone (and how you can apply it): 1. “Think 100 Years Ahead” (But Execute Tomorrow) Patrick famously tells employees: “Assume Stripe will exist for 100+ years. Now, what’s worth doing today?” How this plays out: Relentless focus on infrastructure: While competitors chased quick wins, Stripe spent years building global compliance systems (now used by 150+ countries). No “growth at all costs”: In 2023, Stripe delayed its IPO to avoid “market noise” and doubled down on profitability. Balance audacious vision with ruthless prioritization. Ask: “Will this decision matter in 5 years?” 2. The “Engineer as CEO” MentalityPatrick (a self-taught coder) leads like a systems architect — scaling culture through principles, not policies. Example: Stripe’s “Write Everything Down” Rule Every major decision is documented in Canonical (Stripe’s internal wiki) Engineers write “bug post-mortems” — Patrick applies this to leadership, publishing “decision autopsies” on failed bets. Why it works: Transparency eliminates tribal knowledge. New hires onboard faster, and missteps become team learnings. 3. The “Talent Density” Obsession Stripe’s hiring bar is Silicon Valley legend. But Patrick’s secret isn’t just hiring A-players — it’s keeping them obsessed. Tactics to steal: “Pre-shipping” projects: Engineers pitch ideas before joining (e.g., a candidate built Stripe’s fraud detection prototype during interviews). No managers for the first 100 hires: Patrick believed early autonomy would attract “self-directed killers.” It worked — Stripe’s first 10 engineers stayed 8+ years. 4. “Default to Open” CommunicationPatrick banned “closed-door strategy sessions” in 2015. How Stripe shares everything: Monthly “Ask Me Anything” docs: Patrick answers 100+ raw questions from employees (e.g., “Why did we cut X feature?”). Customer emails forwarded company-wide: Every complaint/praise is visible, forcing teams to confront blind spots. Result: Stripe’s Glassdoor rating (4.7) beats Google, and attrition is 40% below industry average. 5. The “Arbitrage Learning” Hack Patrick’s reading list for execs (e.g., “Why Nations Fail”) isn’t for show — it’s strategy fuel. His rule: If you solve a problem, share how you learned to solve it. When Stripe struggled with remote work, Patrick studied NASA’s Mars mission team structures. Before expanding to Africa, he spent 3 months interviewing 200+ local founders. Stripe’s $95B valuation isn’t about payments… it’s about leadership that scales thinking, not just headcount. Want to build a company that outlasts market cycles? Follow for regular breakdowns of category-defining CEOs.

Clayton Shivers

Why Software Companies are leaving Stripe: Stripe made payments simple. That’s why everyone started there. But Stripe was built for companies that just need to process transactions, not the ones that want to actually make money on them. When you’re doing real volume, that 2.9 percent plus 30 cents per transaction adds up fast. Unless you’re marking it up, you’re not earning anything from payments. You’re just handing margin to Stripe. That’s why more software companies are rethinking it. They’re realizing that if they already manage the customer, the billing, and the platform, there’s no reason they shouldn’t take a piece of the payment flow too. It’s not about hating Stripe. It’s about outgrowing it. At a certain point, it makes more sense to own the payment layer, keep control of the experience, and share in the margin instead of giving it all away. Stripe is perfect for starting out. Embedded payments are what make sense once you grow up.

The 7 lines of code that turned Stripe into a $95B company (while others failed) In 2010, accepting online payments was so complex it required: • 3+ weeks of engineering time • Multiple bank approvals • Security compliance headaches • Custom integration work Dozens of payment startups were adding features, creating "full-solution platforms" to solve this. But Patrick and John Collison made the opposite decision: They created a payment API requiring just 7 lines of code. When customers asked for features, they refused and doubled down on simplicity. Their "7-line" strategy seemed crazy but created 3 advantages: 1. Developer virality: Engineers shared Stripe because it was "beautiful code" — giving them free distribution when competitors spent millions on sales 2. Integration moat: By controlling the full payment stack (not outsourcing), they delivered 99.99% uptime while competitors suffered outages 3. Enterprise foundation: The simple API actually scaled better for large customers, enabling Stripe to move upmarket while feature-focused competitors got stuck downmarket The results: • 2010: 7-line MVP • 2011: $2M in funding • 2016: Processing billions monthly • 2021: $95B valuation • 2024: Powers >50% of online businesses Meanwhile, dozens of well-funded competitors with "more features" failed. Key founder lesson: Adding features feels like progress but often destroys it. The most valuable startups don't win by doing more things — they win by doing one critical thing 10X better. What's the one core function your product needs to nail before anything else?

Jens Kohnen

Wondering why new features at Stripe take weeks – and at legacy players, still years? The difference isn't team size or resources. It's architectural debt versus architectural advantage. Legacy payment providers built monolithic systems when online payments were simple. Every new feature requires untangling years of interdependent code, extensive testing across rigid integrations, and complex deployment cycles. What should take weeks stretches into quarters or years. Modern composable architectures flip this entirely. Instead of one massive system doing everything, you get modular services that connect flexibly. Need a new payment method? Plug it in. Want to adjust routing logic? Configure, don't rebuild. Developer-friendly APIs replace those painful monolithic integrations that require months of planning. This is exactly why we built Hellgate®'s Composable Payment Architecture (CPA) as our foundation. CPA isn't just about technical elegance – it's about merchant success. When you can deliver new capabilities in weeks instead of years, you're not just keeping up with market demands, you're staying ahead of them. Your conversion rates don't wait for lengthy development cycles. Your customers don't pause their expectations while you untangle legacy code. In payments, speed to market directly impacts revenue. The enterprises winning in today's market aren't just processing transactions – they're adapting faster than their competition. Composable beats complexity. CPA is the architecture advantage of the future. 🚀

Everyone keeps trying to replace Stripe. Then they realize why no one can. Every few months, someone announces they’re building a “Stripe killer.” Then a year later, they quietly migrate back. It’s easy to copy a payment form. It’s impossible to copy an ecosystem. Stripe built more than an API, it built gravity. Payments, billing, stablecoins, treasury, usage tracking, everything flows toward it. Founders complain about fees or bugs, but their invoices, payouts, and customer data all still sit inside Stripe. Pull one thread and the whole stack moves with it. That’s why Stripe never really loses customers. It just waits for them to realize they never left.

The Stripe's Homepage Copy Masterclass Stripe lets have it. Stripe's homepage doesn't say "payment processing." It says: "Financial infrastructure to grow your revenue" Why this crushes: "Payment processing" focuses on commodity and they compete on price while "Financial infrastructure" focuses on Stripe as a platform and they compete on value they provide heavily. The positioning shift: Small thinking: "We help you accept payments" Big thinking: "We're the foundation you can build on" The SaaS lesson that we learn here: Don't describe what you DO. Describe what you ENABLE. Zoom isn't "video calls" It's "work from anywhere" Stripe isn't "payments" It's "internet economy infrastructure" Think bigger. Position bigger.

# Fahim Sachedina’s Post
[![View profile for Fahim Sachedina]()](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/fahimsach?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)
[Fahim Sachedina](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/fahimsach?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)
Strategy & Partnerships @ Optimism | I write about Crypto, Startups, Finance and Mental Models
2mo
Patrick Collison's (co-founder & CEO at[Stripe](https://www.linkedin.com/company/stripe?trk=public_post-text)) advice for people in their 20's: “𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗲𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗵𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗿𝗱𝘀”Looking back, the times I learned the most had one thing in common: 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲𝗜𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗲𝗱𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵.
Not just their intelligence, but:
→Did they challenge me?
→Did they push me to grow?
→Did they create an environment where excellence was the norm?
The higher the standards around me, the higher my own baseline became. Those experiences rewired my expectations of what "great work" really looks like.
As Patrick says: “One way to think about your 20s is to explore different domains—but more importantly, find the places where the highest standards exist. Where can you go to experience excellence firsthand?”
Where have you worked that raised your personal standards the most?

##### **Transcript**
There's interesting book Apprentice to Genius that follows or it's 3 or 4. It's 3 generations of scientists. So you know, someone who mentored somebody else who in turn mentored another scientist. And they're all extremely successful. And the book is kind of this, this, this reflection on, I mean, this, this description of how they all did, but also this kind of reflection about like, what is it that was transferred? And you know, for example, one thing it describes is well. And the most important and subtle, you know, questions in sciences, problem selection, like just how do you choose what to work on, right? No, no, no one tells you what to do. And, and, and you do have to answer this question multiple times, like with the company, in some sense, you have to decide like once and then, you know, it's kind of, well, maybe it's an iterative process from there was in science you're, you're frequently pursuing, you know, completely new problems. And of course you choose something that's, you know, sufficiently important and hard that would be important if you succeeded, but also that. That, you know, it's not so intractable that, you know, you can't actually make any progress. And so the the book describes how this is part of what the mentees described, that, you know, they learned from their mentors. Another thing they talk about is just learning about high standards and what high standards actually are. And when I talk to people in other domains, this is so frequently the thing that I hear from them that when they worked with X person or. Why your organization or in the environment or whatever that they learned what great actually is and that just permanently changed their sense for what their what their own standard for their work ought to be and so. And so maybe one version of the, you know, people in the 20s should do is is, you know. Get get some ideas to domain you're interested in or care about, but then figure out where can you learn the highest standards, Where are the highest standards embodied and where you know where where where can you go and experience that first hand.

My interview with Jony Ive at Stripe Sessions: https://lnkd.in/g6J5D9Nm. He was really terrific.
40 Comments
Like Comment
Evan Reiss 10mo
It was SUCH a refreshing talk. Honestly I loved his position remembering why we do design/creative in the first place. The world has forced creatives to constantly look at the measurable and attributions. That’s great for ROI, not so much for creative innovation. I would love to see design and creative shift away from the objective for a little bit and back into something exploratory. Creatives are at risk losing their muse when they are constantly in a position of “decision-defensive fatigue.”
